title: Apple tarte Tatin
subtitle: Caramelised apples baked under puff pastry, inverted
excerpt: The French inverted apple tart: apples cooked in butter and sugar to a deep caramel in an ovenproof pan, topped with puff pastry, baked, and turned out so the caramelised apples are on top. One 23 cm Tatin, serving six to eight.
sourceNotes: The Tatin sisters of the Hôtel Tatin in Lamotte-Beuvron (late nineteenth century) are credited with the inverted tart's accidental creation; the form became established in French household cookery in the early twentieth century. Marie Ébrard, La Cuisinière de la campagne et de la ville (pre-1928, public domain in France): documents the caramel-apples-and-pastry technique. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): notes the inverted apple tart as a French method recognised in British households.

content.text: Tarte Tatin is built upside-down in a heavy ovenproof pan. Butter and sugar cook to a deep amber caramel. Apple halves go into the caramel cut-side up and cook until soft and glossy. A disc of puff pastry is laid over the apples and tucked down. The whole pan bakes until the pastry is risen and golden. Then the tart is flipped onto a serving plate so the apples are on top.

Use a firm dessert apple that holds its shape: Cox, Braeburn, or russet. Cooking apples collapse to apple sauce; eating apples that hold structure are correct here. A heavy pan with a metal handle is essential, the pan goes into the oven, and a plastic handle will melt.

items.name: Eating apple
items.prepNote: about 6 medium Cox, Braeburn or russet, peeled, cored, halved

items.name: Puff pastry
items.prepNote: all-butter, rolled to a 25 cm round, 3 mm thick

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Squeeze the {{lemon}} over the {{apple-eating}} halves to stop them browning. Put the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} into the cast-iron pan over medium heat. Stir until the butter melts and the sugar starts to bubble. Cook 4 to 5 minutes more without stirring; swirl the pan; cook until the caramel turns deep amber. This is the
stage. Take off the heat. Arrange the apple halves cut-side up in a tight spiral in the caramel, packing them in.
Cook the apples, lay the pastry

Return to a medium heat for 15 minutes; the apples should soften and burnish on the underside. Lift the pan off the heat. Lay the rolled {{puff-pastry}} over the apples, tucking the edges down inside the pan around the fruit.

Bake for 25 to 30 minutes; the pastry should be deep gold and risen. Rest the pan on a heatproof surface for 10 minutes. Place a serving plate over the pan, hold both firmly, and turn the whole thing over in one steady movement. Lift the pan away; the apples should be glossy with caramel on top of the puff. Serve warm with crème fraîche or vanilla ice cream.
